title: Unjustified .unwrap() calls in non-test production code
description:
Multiple .unwrap() calls in production code paths without justification or .expect() messages:

1. src/content.rs:6 — HASHTAG_RE regex .unwrap() — justified (known-good literal), but should use .expect('valid regex') for clarity per standards.
2. src/content.rs:10 — MENTION_RE regex .unwrap() — same.
3. src/content.rs:15 — URL_RE regex .unwrap() — same.
4. src/media.rs:229 — blurhash::encode(...).unwrap_or_default() — acceptable (graceful fallback).
5. src/server.rs:49-58 — Multiple header .parse().unwrap() calls in security_headers middleware. These are string literals so they cannot fail, but .expect() with a note would document why.
6. src/delivery.rs:189 — serde_json::to_value(t).unwrap_or_default() — acceptable fallback.
7. src/feed.rs:198 — reqwest::Client::builder()...build().unwrap_or_default() — acceptable fallback.
8. src/delivery.rs:325 — chrono::Duration::from_std(delay).unwrap_or(...) — acceptable fallback.

The LazyLock regex unwraps (items 1-3) are the most important to address. If a regex literal is wrong, the program panics at first use with no indication of which regex failed. Use .expect('HASHTAG_RE regex') to make the panic message diagnostic.

Impact: Unclear panic messages in production if invariant assumptions are violated.
close_reason: LazyLock regex .unwrap() replaced with .expect() with descriptive message in content.rs. Remaining .unwrap() calls are on infallible operations (header parsing of static strings).
